task: Observe flow of materials and listen for machine malfunctions, such as jamming or spillage, and notify supervisors if corrective actions fail.
human_time_rationale: Continuous monitoring task; time represents typical intervention interval when anomaly is detected and reported to supervisor.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can monitor via vision/audio sensors and detect jamming/spillage patterns; limited by: real-time anomaly classification robustness, judging when corrective actions have "failed" (requires contextual reasoning), and safe decision-making about escalation thresholds.

task: Lay out and fabricate metal structural parts such as plates, bulkheads, and frames.
human_time_rationale: Laying out and fabricating a single structural part (plate, bulkhead, or frame) including measurement, cutting, welding, and finishing: approximately 3 hours for a competent fabricator.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision measurement, marking, oxy-fuel/arc welding, metal cutting (torch, plasma, shear), heavy material handling, and quality inspection. Robots lack safe arc-welding capability, precise layout on varied stock, and adaptive problem-solving for warping/defects.

task: Set up, operate, or tend plating or coating machines to coat metal or plastic products with chromium, zinc, copper, cadmium, nickel, or other metal to protect or decorate surfaces.
human_time_rationale: Setting up and operating an electroplating machine: loading parts into racks, adjusting bath chemistry/temperature, monitoring plating cycles, unloading and inspection. One instance = one batch/job cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy machinery with precise chemical/temperature control, hazardous liquid handling (toxic metal salts, acids), safety interlocks, and real-time monitoring. Limited humanoid dexterity and chemical handling capability. Task-specific automation exists but not general-purpose robots.

task: Monitor machine operations to determine whether adjustments are necessary, stopping machines when problems occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = monitoring a machine during one operational cycle or shift period, detecting a problem, and stopping it. Typical 10–20 min depending on machine complexity and problem type.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perceive some visual/auditory anomalies and execute stop commands, but struggle with: diagnosis nuance, unpredictable failure modes, fast reactions to safety-critical events, and generalizing across diverse equipment designs.

task: Operate and maintain laser plate-making equipment that converts electronic data to plates without the use of film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setup, load substrate, run plate-making cycle, unload finished plate. Typical cycle 20–30 min plus setup/teardown.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise substrate alignment, real-time laser parameter tuning, quality inspection, and troubleshooting. Current robots lack the fine optical alignment, industrial-environment perception, and deep domain expertise needed for reliable operation and maintenance.

task: Inspect track for defects such as broken rails and switch malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: Inspection of a typical 1–2 km track section, including visual checks and testing switch mechanisms. One instance = one assigned section.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat rails and perform basic visual inspection, but identifying defects (hairline cracks, wear, alignment issues) requires fine perception. Testing switches requires dexterity and understanding of mechanical resistance. Safety-critical task demands high reliability. Outdoor environment and variable lighting add challenges.

task: Test and charge batteries.
human_time_rationale: Testing a battery (voltage check, load test) and charging takes 10–20 min per unit depending on charger type and battery capacity. One instance = one battery.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can locate and grasp batteries, but safe charging requires precise connector alignment, understanding of polarity/safety interlocks, and ability to interpret test readings (voltmeter, load tester). Risk of fire/injury if mishandled limits autonomy.

task: Report any unusual circumstances concerning mail delivery, including the condition of street letter boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and reporting on mail delivery issues and condition of letter boxes for a typical residential block or mail route segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires mobile navigation, identifying mailbox damage/vandalism, assessing mail condition, and generating reports. Robot can traverse sidewalks and detect visual anomalies, but struggles with nuanced condition assessment, judgment of "unusual," and accessing recessed/elevated boxes.

task: Provide assistance to workers engaged in construction or extraction activities, using hand tools or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one 8-hour work day assisting on a construction or extraction site. Includes material handling, tool passing, setup/breakdown, and basic labor tasks.
robot_success_rationale: Limited by: unstructured sites, hazard navigation, varied terrain (rough/muddy), heavy load-bearing, unpredictable task sequencing, site-specific tool adaptation. Humanoids lack strength and reliability in outdoor/dusty conditions. Perception of hazards (falling objects, excavation) inadequate.

task: Assemble and cement sections of stained glass together.
human_time_rationale: Assembling and cementing a typical stained glass panel (~1–2 sq ft) with 8–15 pieces, including layout, alignment, and cure time monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of fragile glass pieces, controlled cement application, and fine manipulation. Current robots lack dexterity, force control, and visual feedback precision needed for glass assembly without breakage.

task: Remove fixtures such as pictures, door knobs, lamps, or electric switch covers prior to painting.
human_time_rationale: Removing 8–12 typical fixtures (pictures, doorknobs, light switch covers, outlet covers, lamps) from an average room, including unscrewing, unbolting, and careful handling. One instance = one room's fixtures.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (unscrewing small fasteners, handling delicate items), precise tool use (screwdrivers), spatial reasoning to avoid damaging walls/paint, and careful part tracking. Current robots struggle with small screw manipulation and fragile object handling.

task: Remove asbestos or lead from surfaces, using hand or power tools such as scrapers, vacuums, or high-pressure sprayers.
human_time_rationale: Removing asbestos/lead from a typical room-sized area (e.g., stripping paint from walls, ceiling, or fixtures). Includes setup, careful scraping, vacuuming containment, and cleanup. One instance = one room or major surface.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazardous material handling protocols, precise tool control to avoid dispersing fibers/dust into air, secure containment, and coordination with regulatory/safety standards. Current humanoids lack specialized safety systems, fine hazmat control, and liability frameworks for toxic exposure work. Not a deployment-ready task.

task: Assemble and install communication equipment such as data and telephone communication lines, wiring, switching equipment, wiring frames, power apparatus, computer systems, and networks.
human_time_rationale: One instance: assemble and install a complete communication cabinet or rack with wiring, switching equipment, and network connections. Includes planning, component positioning, cable routing, termination, and testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise cable routing, connector termination (RJ45, fiber splicing), spatial reasoning in constrained racks, and quality validation. Current dexterity inadequate for fine wiring work; perception challenges with connector alignment and cable management in complex 3D layouts.

task: Clean switches and replace contact points, using vacuum hoses, solvents, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and replacing contact points on one electrical switch assembly, including vacuum cleaning, solvent application, contact removal, inspection, and reinstallation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (contact point removal/replacement), precise solvent application control, vacuum hose operation, and quality verification. Limited by poor fine-motor precision, difficulty handling small frangible components, and inability to detect contact condition visually/tactilely.

task: Test battery charges, and replace or recharge batteries as necessary.
human_time_rationale: Test a battery (multimeter check ~2 min), then recharge or replace (~10 min). One instance = one battery cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can locate and manipulate battery packs in some structured environments, but lack reliable perception to test voltage/condition, safe handling of hazardous batteries, and judgment for replacement decisions. Dexterity for connectors varies.

task: Lubricate wearing surfaces of mechanical parts, using oils or other lubricants.
human_time_rationale: Applying lubricant to accessible wearing surfaces on a typical assembly of mechanical parts (bearings, hinges, joints). Includes locating parts, selecting appropriate lubricant, and applying it.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate to parts and dispense lubricants, but struggle with: identifying correct lubricant type, reaching confined spaces, controlling application rate, and avoiding over/under-lubrication. Perception of wear and judgment required.

task: Trace and tag meters or house lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing and tagging one utility line (water, gas, electric, or sewer) from source to endpoint, typically 50–150 feet. Includes visual inspection, labeling, and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor mobility over varied terrain, accurate line identification under occlusion/burial, fine manipulation to attach tags, and safe navigation near live utilities. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured outdoor navigation, visual tracing through soil/conduit, and reliable tag placement.

task: Vary air pressure flowing into regulators and turn handles to assess functioning of valves and pistons.
human_time_rationale: Testing air pressure settings and handle operation on one regulator/valve assembly; includes pressure dial adjustment, handle movement, and functional assessment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise pressure dial adjustment (moderate dexterity), handle turning (achievable), and interpretation of pressure gauges/piston response. Perception of gauge readings and dial feedback is the limiting factor; fine pressure tuning challenging.

task: Cut, strip, and bend wire leads at ends of coils, using pliers and wire scrapers.
human_time_rationale: Cutting, stripping, and bending wire leads on one coil involves sequential hand operations with simple tools. A skilled worker completes this in 3–7 minutes per coil.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise dexterity (holding coil, positioning pliers/scraper, bending to spec). Wire diameter/material variability and achieving correct bend angle/length without damage or insulation tears is challenging for current gripper control and tactile sensing.

task: Tend machines that bond cope and drag together to form completed shell molds.
human_time_rationale: Positioning cope/drag halves, activating bonding process, inspecting alignment. One instance = one mold shell assembly cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial alignment of two heavy components, fine manual adjustment, tool operation, and quality inspection. Current robots lack fine dexterity and real-time error correction for mold tolerance.

task: Select lumber to be used for patterns.
human_time_rationale: Worker visually inspects lumber stock, checks grain/quality, and selects pieces meeting pattern specifications. One instance = selecting lumber for one pattern project.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual assessment of wood grain, knots, moisture, color matching, and judgment of suitability. Robots lack fine visual discrimination and domain-specific lumber grading knowledge. High failure rate on quality criteria.

task: Tighten pulleys or add abrasives to maintain cutting speeds.
human_time_rationale: Tightening pulleys and replacing abrasives on cutting equipment typically takes 15–25 min depending on equipment access and wear severity. One instance = one maintenance cycle per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Requires tool manipulation (wrenches, hex keys), torque sensing to avoid over-tightening, identification of wear, and safe handling of sharp abrasive materials. Current humanoids lack fine torque control and specialized perception for wear assessment.

task: Grade, classify, or sort products according to sizes, weights, colors, or other specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting/grading a batch of ~50–100 products by size, weight, or color. Typical quality control or warehouse sorting task taking 10–20 minutes per batch.
robot_success_rationale: Robots excel at pick-and-place (~70–90%) and can classify by size/weight via sensors. However, color/shade discrimination and handling varied product geometries remain challenging. Generalization to new product types is weak. Semi-structured tasks on a conveyor or bin perform better than fully unstructured.

task: Clean semiconductor wafers using cleaning equipment, such as chemical baths, automatic wafer cleaners, or blow-off wands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a batch of wafers (typically 25–50 wafers per cassette) through chemical baths and drying. Includes loading, processing, unloading, inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, chemical safety awareness, delicate wafer manipulation to avoid contamination/breakage, and mastery of specialized equipment sequencing. Humanoids lack the fine control and chemical safety protocols; perception of wafer cleanliness is difficult. High risk of damage.

task: Place products in containers according to grade and mark grades on containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and grading ~50–100 items into 3–5 grade categories and marking each container. Typical packing/sorting role benchmark.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reliable visual grading assessment (variable condition/quality perception), precise placement into containers (moderate dexterity), and accurate labeling/marking. Perception and fine marking are bottlenecks; generalization to product variation is weak.

task: Fit and fasten wallboard or drywall into position on wood or metal frameworks, using glue, nails, or screws.
human_time_rationale: A competent drywall worker fastens one 4×8-ft sheet to wall framing with ~15–20 fasteners (nails or screws), including positioning, driving, and spacing. One instance = one sheet installed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise positioning of heavy awkward sheets, aligning edges, driving fasteners straight and evenly spaced, handling power tools safely. Current robots lack dexterity, strength coordination, and force feedback for consistent quality drywall installation.

task: Move or position materials such as marble slabs, using cranes, hoists, or dollies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = positioning one marble slab from point A to point B using crane/hoist/dolly. Includes setup, maneuvering, securing, and final placement on a work surface or staging area.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate cranes/hoists safely or legally. Requires heavy equipment control, spatial reasoning under load, and precise coordinated positioning. Autonomous systems exist only as fixed installations (bolted arms), not mobile units. Risk of material/personnel damage extremely high.

task: Observe leveling indicator arms to verify levelness and alignment of tracks.
human_time_rationale: Visual inspection of leveling indicators on equipment tracks; one instance = checking one piece of equipment or one set of tracks for levelness and alignment.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perceive and analyze visual indicators via cameras, but must reliably identify arm positions, interpret indicator scales, and make judgment calls on acceptable alignment tolerances. Perception of small indicators and spatial reasoning about levelness requires stable positioning and good lighting.

task: Perform pumping tests to assess well performance.
human_time_rationale: One pumping test (setup, monitoring flow/pressure over time, data logging): ~2 hours for a competent water technician including site inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operation of specialized well-testing equipment (pumps, flow meters, pressure gauges), field-specific judgment on anomalies, precise sensor reading and adjustment. Humanoids lack dexterity for delicate instrument calibration and cannot safely operate high-pressure field equipment autonomously.

task: Clean and prepare sites for excavation or boring.
human_time_rationale: Clearing debris, marking boundaries, setting up equipment on a typical excavation site. One instance = one job site ready for machinery operation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy machinery (excavators, dozers), navigating unstructured outdoor terrain with hazards, interpreting safety protocols. Humanoids lack capacity to operate equipment, navigate rough/variable ground reliably, or assess site-specific safety constraints.

task: Test and replace electrical components and wiring, using test meters, soldering equipment, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnose, test, replace one electrical component or wiring section (e.g., swap a resistor/capacitor, solder a joint, replace a section of wire). Includes testing and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine soldering dexterity (<5%), precise component identification, multimeter operation, and context-dependent decision-making. Current humanoids lack the hand precision, thermal management, and electrical safety awareness needed for reliable autonomous soldering and component-level repair.

task: Observe and test operation of appliances following installation, and make any initial installation adjustments that are necessary.
human_time_rationale: Observing appliance operation and making minor adjustments post-installation. One instance = one appliance. Typically 20–40 min depending on appliance type and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual inspection of appliance behavior, understanding of expected operation, fine motor adjustments (leveling, valve tweaking), and contextual knowledge of appliance-specific setup. Robots can observe but struggle with diagnosis, dexterity, and judgment calls on acceptability.

task: Inspect and test docks, ships, buoyage systems, plant intakes or outflows, or underwater pipelines, cables, or sewers, using closed circuit television, still photography, and testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One complete inspection of a dock section or pipeline segment, including positioning equipment, survey work, reviewing footage, and documentation. Assumes 1–2 hours per site.
robot_success_rationale: Requires underwater/maritime navigation, precise camera positioning, interpreting visual anomalies, and specialized testing equipment operation. Humanoids lack waterproofing, underwater dexterity, and autonomy in unstructured underwater environments. Stationary inspection rigs score higher; mobile robots do not.

task: Cut and weld steel, using underwater welding equipment, jigs, and supports.
human_time_rationale: Cutting and welding a steel joint underwater; one instance = one weld seam or joint. Includes positioning, equipment setup, welding time, and inspection.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate underwater; require specialized pressurized suits, breathing systems, and heavy specialized equipment. Welding demands precision, thermal feedback, and real-time arc monitoring. Extreme environmental constraints prohibit autonomous operation.

task: Mask off mold areas not to be laminated, using cellophane, wax paper, masking tape, or special sprays containing mold-release substances.
human_time_rationale: Masking a typical composite mold (e.g., for boat hull or automotive part) with tape, paper, and spray, covering edges and protected zones. One instance = one mold setup.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise masking around complex mold geometry, applying adhesive tape without wrinkles, and careful spray application. Current robots lack fine object handling, surface perception, and planning for irregular contours.

task: Unwind lengths of yarn, thread, or twine from spools and wind onto bobbins.
human_time_rationale: One instance: unwinding and winding one spool of yarn/thread onto one bobbin. Competent worker completes in ~5–10 min depending on spool size and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation (thread/yarn is fragile), precise tension control, and reliable bobbin engagement. Current dexterity and perception of thin, flexible materials remain weak. High risk of tangling or breakage.

task: Fit, install, and secure material on frames, using hand tools, power tools, glue, cement, or staples.
human_time_rationale: Fitting material to frame (e.g., fabric to stretcher, drywall to studs) typically takes 20–30 min per unit, including measuring, positioning, fastening with tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment, force control for fastening, tool switching (hammer, stapler, glue application), and judgment of material fit—challenges in dexterity, tool coordination, and perceptual feedback.

task: Perform tests to check the qualities and grades of products, such as assessing levels of bottom sediment, water, and foreign materials in oil samples, using centrifugal testers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one oil sample batch through centrifugal test (sample prep, machine loading, spinning, reading results). Typical lab procedure takes 15–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise sample handling, centrifuge operation, visual or optical result reading. Robot can load/unload samples (~60%), but interpreting sediment/water levels requires fine visual judgment and occasional recalibration. Centrifuge safety (containment, spinning hazards) further limits autonomous deployment.

task: Count or weigh and bundle items.
human_time_rationale: Counting/weighing small items and bundling typically takes 5–10 min per bundle of 20–50 items, depending on size and binding method.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place items (~70%) but counting requires reliable vision, weighing needs scale integration, and bundling (tying, wrapping) requires dexterity and force control—limited at ~30–50% for varied items.

task: Mark cutting lines around patterns or templates, or follow layout points, using squares, rules, and straightedges, and chalk, pencils, or scribes.
human_time_rationale: Marking lines on a single workpiece (e.g., fabric, wood, metal) using templates/rules/straightedges; includes measuring and marking multiple reference points and lines around a pattern.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spatial reasoning, stable handling of straightedge/template, fine motor control to hold marking tool at correct angle/pressure. Robot perception of marking points and template alignment is moderately reliable; inconsistent pressure control and tool orientation limit success on varied materials.

task: Unroll, lay out, attach, or mount materials or items on cutting tables or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unroll and mount one item (e.g., fabric bolt, sheet material) on a cutting table or machine, including alignment and basic fastening. Assumes standard materials and accessible mounting points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires unrolling/unfurling flexible materials (dexterity challenge), precise positioning and alignment on a table/machine, and fastening (clamps, tape, or fixtures). Current humanoids struggle with coordinated manipulation of large, deformable items and tool-use precision alignment.

task: Perform general labor duties such as repairing booms.
human_time_rationale: Repairing a boom (construction/crane equipment) typically requires diagnosis, part removal/replacement, and testing. One instance = one complete boom repair job.
robot_success_rationale: Boom repair demands heavy tool operation (wrenches, torque tools), structural diagnostics, and safe re-assembly of load-bearing equipment. Humanoids lack the strength, specialized tool proficiency, and safety judgment for autonomous operation on heavy machinery.

task: Operate power-driven pumps that transfer liquids, semi-liquids, gases, or powdered materials.
human_time_rationale: Typical instance: start pump, monitor flow/pressure gauges, adjust settings, verify transfer complete. One transfer operation (e.g., one tank or batch).
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack reliable fluid/powder leak detection, pressure-gauge reading/interpretation, and safe shutdown under fault conditions. Hose attachment/detachment is dexterous. No autonomous error recovery.

task: Assemble or adjust parts or related electrical units of prototypes to prepare for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling and adjusting electrical components of one prototype for testing. Involves positioning, fastening, wiring, and calibration tasks typical of electronics prototyping work.
robot_success_rationale: Moderate dexterity needed for small connectors, wiring, and fasteners. Perception of circuit board layouts and component orientation challenging. Fine motor control for adjustments limited. Can handle standardized parts on jigs better than ad-hoc prototype assembly. Electrical safety awareness also a constraint.

task: Provide adapted physical education services to students with intellectual disabilities, autism, traumatic brain injury, orthopedic impairments, or other disabling condition.
human_time_rationale: One class period: planning, instructing, and adapting exercises for a group of students with varied disabilities; typically 40–50 minutes of direct contact per session.
robot_success_rationale: Requires understanding individual cognitive/motor needs, real-time behavioral adjustment, emotional support, safety monitoring of vulnerable students, and adaptive teaching—all beyond current robot capability. Fine motor demonstration, safety intervention, and empathetic interaction remain infeasible.

task: Diagnose infections, such as Hepatitis B and Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS), by conducting tests to detect the antibodies that patients' immune systems make to fight such infections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = conducting and interpreting diagnostic tests (blood draw, sample preparation, assay execution, result review) for one patient screening for multiple infections. Competent lab technician: ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile phlebotomy (venipuncture), precise liquid handling with biohazard risk, calibration of lab instruments, and clinical decision-making. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity, sterile technique capability, and safety protocols for bloodborne pathogen handling. Below 10% success.

task: Assess birthing environments to ensure cleanliness, safety, and the availability of appropriate supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = thorough pre-delivery assessment of a birth suite (hospital/clinic room), including visual inspection, equipment check, supply inventory, safety compliance verification. Competent medical/nursing staff typically require 30–60 min depending on facility size.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate and perceive (camera, LIDAR) to detect gross cleanliness issues and identify supplies. However, this task requires nuanced safety judgments (sterile protocols, equipment functionality verification, medical compliance interpretation) and high-stakes decision-making. Fine object recognition of medical supplies and condition assessment are challenging. Human oversight essential.

task: Prepare or serve hot or cold beverages, such as coffee, espresso drinks, blended coffees, or teas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = prepare and serve one beverage (coffee, espresso, blended coffee, or tea), from order to handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise liquid handling, temperature control, steam/foam management, and safe handoff. Current robots lack fine dexterity for espresso tamping, milk steaming, and collision-free carrying hot liquids in crowded spaces.

task: Install active solar systems, including solar collectors, concentrators, pumps, or fans.
human_time_rationale: Installing a complete active solar system (collectors, concentrators, pumps, fans) on a typical residential/small commercial roof takes 6-8 hours for experienced technicians. One instance = one full system installation.
robot_success_rationale: Requires roofing work at height (safety/mobility limits), precise fitting of heavy collectors, electrical/plumbing integration, load calculations, and specialized tool operation (torque wrenches, sealants). Current humanoids lack height stability, fine force control, and electrical safety certification.

task: Apply weather seal, such as pipe flashings and sealants, to roof penetrations and structural devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, cleaning, and weather-sealing a typical roof penetration (vent, chimney, or antenna). Includes prep, flashing installation, and sealant application.
robot_success_rationale: Requires: roof mobility on varied slopes/surfaces (30–40% success), precise object manipulation (flashing alignment, fastening), sealant application (fine control), site-specific judgment. Most frontier robots lack stable footing and dexterous control needed for this outdoor trade task.

task: Operate equipment to start, stop, or regulate biomass-fueled generators, generator units, boilers, engines, or auxiliary systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = starting, stopping, or regulating a biomass generator/boiler system through control panels/switches. Typical startup/shutdown cycle including safety checks and adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids cannot operate specialized industrial control interfaces reliably. Requires reading analog/digital gauges, turning valves, pressing context-specific buttons, understanding safety interlocks, and responding to equipment states—beyond current perception and dexterity generalization.

task: Develop laser-processed designs, such as laser-cut medical devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance: design, program, and execute laser-cutting of one custom medical device prototype. Includes CAD setup, laser parameter tuning, and quality check.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack design creativity, CAD expertise, and specialized laser equipment operation skills. Cannot autonomously program laser parameters, troubleshoot material interactions, or validate medical compliance. Humanoid cannot directly operate precision laser tools safely.

task: Set up, operate, or monitor invasive equipment and devices, such as colostomy or tracheotomy equipment, mechanical ventilators, catheters, gastrointestinal tubes, and central lines.
human_time_rationale: Setup/monitoring of one invasive device (e.g., catheter placement, tracheotomy care, ventilator settings adjustment, feeding tube management, central line dressing change). Varies by device; 15 min is a typical clinical instance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires sterile technique, anatomical precision, real-time patient assessment, and response to emergencies. Current robots lack fine tactile control, infection-prevention training, and critical decision-making. Inappropriate for autonomous deployment.

task: Obtain specimens or samples for laboratory work.
human_time_rationale: Median time to collect and prepare one sample (e.g., blood draw, swab, tissue biopsy, or environmental sample), including labeling and documentation.
robot_success_rationale: Robots lack fine dexterity and sterile technique for invasive procedures (blood draws, biopsies). Can retrieve pre-labeled samples from shelves or transport labeled containers, but cannot perform safe, accurate collection on living subjects or complex environmental sampling.

task: Transport specimens, laboratory items, or pharmacy items, ensuring proper documentation and delivery to authorized personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collect items, verify documentation, navigate facility, deliver to authorized personnel. Typical round trip in a hospital/lab setting, including verification and handoff.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate structured indoor environments and carry loads; however, restricted-access areas (clean rooms, secure labs), authentication with personnel, handling temperature-sensitive/fragile specimens, and interpreting authorization protocols remain challenging. Documentation verification requires reliable perception.

task: Train other medical personnel in phlebotomy or laboratory techniques.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one training session covering phlebotomy or lab technique for a single trainee or small group (1–4 people), including demonstration, observation, and feedback.
robot_success_rationale: Requires complex social interaction, real-time assessment of trainee understanding, adaptive feedback, fine motor demonstration (needle insertion, sterile technique), and judgment. Current robots cannot reliably teach, assess competence, or adjust instruction in real-time.

task: Inspect completed work to ensure all hardware is tight, antennas are level, hangers are properly fastened, proper support is in place, or adequate weather proofing has been installed.
human_time_rationale: Typical inspection of a completed hardware installation (antenna/hanger system). One instance = one unit or structure (e.g. one satellite dish, antenna array, or hanger assembly).
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual inspection, torque-check assessment (fastener tightness is subtle), levelness judgment, and environmental assessment. Robots can perceive and classify mounting hardware, but cannot reliably assess tension/tightness without tactile feedback. Weather-proofing verification demands detailed material inspection.

task: Perform regular maintenance of laboratory equipment by inspecting, calibrating, cleaning, or sterilizing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = complete maintenance cycle on one piece of equipment (inspect, calibrate, clean, sterilize). Varies by equipment complexity; 45 min represents typical analytical/medical lab instrument.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for calibration adjustment, precise perception to detect drift/contamination, knowledge of equipment-specific protocols, and safe handling of sterilization agents. Humanoids lack calibration accuracy and generalization to diverse instruments.

task: Measure or weigh ingredients used in laboratory testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance: measure/weigh a standard set of 3–5 ingredients for a typical lab procedure. Includes reading scales, recording values, minor material handling.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick up containers and place them on scales with moderate dexterity. Challenge: reading analog/digital displays reliably, handling small containers without spillage, precision placement on balance pans, recipe interpretation. Some vision systems can read digital outputs; weight accuracy tolerance unknown.

task: Assist mechanics and perform various mechanical duties, such as changing oil or checking and replacing batteries.
human_time_rationale: Typical oil change + battery inspection/replacement on one vehicle; includes draining, refilling, checking terminals, swapping battery if needed.
robot_success_rationale: Requires accessing undercarriage (moderate mobility), unscrewing varied fasteners (moderate dexterity), handling hot/greasy components safely, and precise fluid handling. Battery swap is simpler (~50%) but oil changes involve splash risk, torque sensitivity, and hazardous waste. Average ~25%.

task: Assist in bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assist one passenger with boarding/alighting (help with luggage, balance, mobility aid). Typical duration 1–3 minutes per passenger.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe human physical contact, understanding passenger mobility needs, variable luggage handling, and confined bus spaces. Limited by dexterity with soft loads, perception of passenger stability needs, and safety risk in close quarters.

task: Sweep or vacuum the cremation chamber to retrieve remains for storage in an urn or other container.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sweeping/vacuuming one cremation chamber after a cremation cycle, collecting all remains. Includes careful debris collection, scooping, and initial placement for transfer to urn.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine perception of ash/bone fragments on uneven chamber surfaces, delicate handling to avoid loss or contamination, and precise transfer to container. High risk of failure due to fragility, thermal residue detection, and generalization to varied chamber geometries and ash distributions.

task: Measure and mark cutting lines on materials, using a ruler, pencil, chalk, and marking gauge.
human_time_rationale: A competent human measures and marks one cutting line or a set of lines on a single workpiece, using ruler/gauge and pencil/chalk, typically 2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine visual perception of ruler/gauge placement, precise pencil/chalk control, and accurate spatial reasoning. Current robots struggle with consistent line straightness, marking pressure, and tight tolerances. Perception and fine dexterity are limiting factors.

task: Verify trueness of structure, using plumb bob and level.
human_time_rationale: Checking a typical structure (wall, column, or frame) for plumb and level, including setup and multiple measurement points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise manipulation of plumb bob and level tools, interpreting bubble/string alignment, and spatial judgment. Current robots lack fine dexterity and reliable perception of subtle level indicators in varied lighting.

task: Guide and stop cars by switching, applying brakes, or placing scotches, or wooden wedges, between wheels and rails.
human_time_rationale: Guiding one train car, switching tracks, and securing with scotches/wedges typically takes 10–20 minutes depending on the rail yard setup and complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy switches, understanding rail dynamics, real-time hazard awareness near moving trains, precise wedge placement under wheels, and unpredictable environment. Current humanoids lack the specialized training, safety protocols, and reliable perception for this critical safety-sensitive task.

task: Connect and turn regulator valves to activate and adjust gas flow and pressure so that desired flames are obtained.
human_time_rationale: Connecting regulator valves and adjusting gas flow/pressure for flame control typically takes a trained operator 3–7 minutes per setup instance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation of small valve controls, real-time visual feedback of flame characteristics, and pressure/flow monitoring. Humanoids lack fine dexterity and precision pressure sensing; safety-critical task with risk of gas leak or flame loss.

task: Inspect locomotives to detect damaged or worn parts.
human_time_rationale: Thorough inspection of one locomotive exterior and accessible undercarriage, including visual checks for corrosion, cracks, wear on wheels, couplers, and brake components.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate around locomotive and detect obvious visual defects via camera. Limited by: inability to access tight undercarriage spaces safely, difficulty classifying subtle wear vs. acceptable aging, and lack of tactile feedback to detect loose bolts or internal play in bearing assemblies.

task: Examine patients to diagnose oral health conditions and diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = comprehensive oral examination of one patient, including visual inspection, palpation, chart documentation. Typical dental checkup duration.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral perception, gentle manipulation inside mouth, subjective clinical judgment, palpation sensitivity, and interpretation of subtle symptoms. Current robots lack oral dexterity, intraoral vision clarity, tactile feedback precision, and clinical diagnostic reasoning needed for reliable diagnosis.

task: Count money in cash drawers at the beginning of shifts to ensure that amounts are correct and that there is adequate change.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting cash in one drawer at shift start. Competent cashier typically counts denominations and verifies total in 3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of variable denominations, accurate visual perception of currency, counting logic, and handling variable bill orientations. Current robots struggle with fine picking and reliable denomination recognition in real-world lighting. High error risk.

task: Prime and paint repaired surfaces, using paint sprayguns and motorized sanders.
human_time_rationale: Priming and painting a typical repaired surface panel (e.g., automotive door or wall section) including sanding prep, masking, and two coats. One instance = one repair area of ~2–4 sq ft.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise spray control, masking accuracy, surface prep judgment (sanding pressure/grit selection), and fine motor coordination. Current robots lack dexterity and tactile feedback for consistent finish quality. Motorized sander operation and spraygun control are still high-risk autonomy tasks.

task: Fit and secure windows, vinyl roofs, and metal trim to vehicle bodies, using caulking guns, adhesive brushes, and mallets.
human_time_rationale: Fitting one window/trim section with caulking, adhesive, and securing typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity and surface prep; 45 min represents a median unit task.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precision dexterity (caulking gun control, adhesive brush work), fine positioning in constrained spaces, impact tool use (mallet), and real-time adjustment. Humanoids lack sufficient hand-eye coordination and tool control for sealing quality acceptable in vehicle manufacturing.

task: Fabricate or repair counters, benches, partitions, or other wooden structures, such as sheds or outbuildings.
human_time_rationale: Building or repairing a modest wooden structure (e.g., workbench, counter, or small shed section) typically requires 2–4 hours for an experienced carpenter, accounting for measurement, cutting, assembly, and finishing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, multi-step planning, tool sequencing (saw, drill, driver, fastening), fine dexterity for alignment and finishing. Current robots lack dexterity, spatial reasoning, and tool mastery for reliable autonomous carpentry.

task: Load and unload baggage in baggage compartments.
human_time_rationale: Loading or unloading one typical passenger's baggage (2–3 bags) into/from a vehicle or aircraft baggage compartment, including securing or organizing items.
robot_success_rationale: Requires picking varied-shape bags, navigating tight compartments, placement accuracy, and environmental generalization. Current humanoids struggle with unstructured bag grasping, confined-space manipulation, and ensuring stable stacking. Some deployments tolerate low success.

task: Test and measure patient's strength, motor development and function, sensory perception, functional capacity, or respiratory or circulatory efficiency and record data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = complete assessment battery (strength, motor, sensory, functional capacity, respiratory/circulatory tests) for one patient, typically 30–60 minutes depending on patient condition and test depth.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced physical handling, fine sensory discrimination (palpation, proprioceptive feedback), clinical judgment, safe patient interaction, and ability to interpret patient responses. Humanoids lack dexterity, tactile sensitivity, and cognitive-clinical reasoning for autonomous assessment.

task: Operate zoom lenses, changing images according to specifications and rehearsal instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one zoom lens change and framing adjustment per shot during rehearsal. A competent camera operator changes focal length, reframes, and confirms image meets spec in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine motor control of zoom mechanism, real-time visual feedback interpretation, and understanding of aesthetic/technical specifications from verbal/written direction. Humanoids lack dexterity and perceptual judgment needed; focal length confirmation difficult without human oversight.

task: Examine patients to obtain information about their physical condition.
human_time_rationale: Typical patient physical examination (vital signs, palpation, auscultation, range-of-motion checks). One instance = one patient exam.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine palpation, interpretation of patient feedback, stethoscope use, auscultation listening, clinical judgment, and safe handling of vulnerable people. Current humanoids lack tactile sensitivity, medical reasoning, and safe interaction in clinical contexts.

task: Conduct electrocardiogram (EKG), phonocardiogram, echocardiogram, stress testing, or other cardiovascular tests to record patients' cardiac activity, using specialized electronic test equipment, recording devices, or laboratory instruments.
human_time_rationale: Complete one patient's cardiovascular test (e.g., standard 12-lead EKG ~5 min, echocardiogram ~30 min, stress test ~60 min). Average across mix: ~45 min per patient instance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise electrode/sensor placement on patient skin, real-time ultrasound probe manipulation, patient interaction/communication, clinical judgment, and safety protocols. Humanoids lack fine tactile feedback, medical training, and ability to respond to patient distress during stress testing.

task: Perform a variety of duties as requested by client, such as obtaining household supplies or running errands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one errand trip (e.g., grocery store run or household supply pickup). Includes travel, navigation, selection, checkout. Typical trip for competent human worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires autonomous navigation in unpredictable public spaces, item identification/retrieval in retail environments, handling varied payment/checkout systems, and safe vehicle operation or traversal. Current humanoids lack robust public navigation, manipulation in crowded settings, and cannot operate vehicles on public roads.

task: Transport patients to and from treatment areas, using wheelchairs or providing standing support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = transport one patient from one location to another (e.g. waiting room to treatment room). Includes positioning, safety checks, and navigation via typical facility hallway.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate hallways and move wheelchairs, but struggle with patient safety (preventing falls, adapting to patient frailty), uncertain cognitive demands (reading patient condition, responding to distress), and physical assistance (standing support requires body awareness and adaptive strength control). Legal/liability concerns severe.

task: Clean and drive funeral vehicles, such as cars or hearses, in funeral processions.
human_time_rationale: One instance: clean one hearse/vehicle (30–40 min) plus drive one typical funeral procession route (45–50 min, including waiting/idling time).
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate pedals, steering wheels, or complex vehicle controls in unstructured traffic. Cleaning requires safe tool handling and fine judgment. Task requires legal driver certification and public-road autonomous capability—neither achievable by current humanoids.

task: Bolt component parts together to form tower assemblies, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one complete tower from component parts using hand tools (wrenches, etc.). Includes positioning, aligning, and fastening multiple bolted joints.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can handle pick-and-place and operate simple tools, but tower assembly demands precise alignment, torque control, and spatial reasoning in 3D. Humanoids struggle with stable positioning of large structures and multi-step sequencing. Moderate success on standardized, well-jig'd assemblies.

task: Fill processing or cooking containers, such as kettles, rotating cookers, pressure cookers, or vats, with ingredients, by opening valves, by starting pumps or injectors, or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling a single container (kettle, pressure cooker, vat, etc.) with ingredients via valve, pump, or hand. Typical time for a food-service worker or cook.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine coordination: locating fill ports, opening/closing valves, operating pump controls, measuring/pouring by hand. Humanoids lack precise volumetric sensing, can spill, struggle with valve mechanics and varied container geometry. Narrow success in controlled industrial settings only.

task: Drive vehicles to or from workshops or customers' workplaces or homes.
human_time_rationale: Typical trip to customer/workshop: 30–60 min depending on distance. One instance = one one-way trip of typical service call distance (10–20 miles urban/suburban).
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate steering wheels, pedals, or gear shifts. Current autonomous vehicle systems (L4 AVs) exist but are task-specific, geofenced, and not general-purpose robots. No frontier humanoid or mobile manipulator can drive a vehicle in traffic.

task: Maintain or repair agricultural facilities, equipment, or tools to ensure operational readiness, safety, and cleanliness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = routine maintenance/repair of one piece of equipment (e.g., tractor servicing, fence repair, tool cleaning). Varies by scope; 45 min typical for common field equipment maintenance.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosis, specialized tools, heavy equipment operation, fine mechanical work, and environmental variability. Humanoids lack dexterity for detailed repair, tool-switching capability, and cannot operate heavy machinery safely.

task: Cut, trim and shape hair or hairpieces, based on customers' instructions, hair type, and facial features, using clippers, scissors, trimmers and razors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one complete haircut (wash, cut, style) for an adult. A competent barber/stylist typically completes this in 25–40 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise 3D spatial reasoning, real-time adaptation to hair texture/curl, facial symmetry perception, fine dexterous control of scissors/clippers, and safe navigation around face/ears. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, tactile feedback, and dynamic perception to reliably execute this without injury risk.

task: Operate cash registers to receive payments from patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one customer transaction (scan/ring items, receive payment, issue receipt). Competent cashier averages 2-4 min per transaction.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine manipulation (inserting cards, handling cash, touching screen buttons), real-time customer interaction, exception handling (invalid payment, declined cards), and safety around cash/valuables. Current robots struggle with fine dexterity, dynamic HCI, and payment security.

task: Provide a variety of materials and resources for children to explore, manipulate, and use, both in learning activities and in imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = curating, organizing, and setting up a diverse play/learning environment with materials for a classroom or play area. Includes selection, arrangement, safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires understanding child development, safety standards, creative spatial arrangement, and fine motor sorting/placement. Humanoids lack domain knowledge and struggle with judgment calls about what materials suit different ages and learning goals.

task: Perform fire protection or suppression duties, such as constructing fire breaks or disposing of brush.
human_time_rationale: Constructing a fire break or disposing of brush for one property or small area typically takes 3–4 hours for a competent worker, accounting for tool setup, clearing, and material handling.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rugged outdoor mobility, heavy-duty tool operation (chainsaws, brush-clearing equipment), dynamic terrain navigation, situational awareness in smoky/hazardous conditions, and sustained heavy work. Humanoids lack durability, battery life, and tool-use capability for this scale.

task: Monitor functioning of equipment and make necessary modifications to ensure system operates in conformance with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting and modifying one piece of equipment to bring it into conformance. Includes diagnostics, adjustments, and verification.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can perform visual inspection and simple adjustments, but lack fine diagnostic reasoning, complex troubleshooting, and ability to safely operate unfamiliar equipment controls and specialized adjustment tools.

task: Design and fabricate dental prostheses, or supervise dental technicians and laboratory bench workers who construct the devices.
human_time_rationale: Design and fabrication of a single complete denture or prosthesis typically spans 6–8 hours across multiple steps (casting, grinding, polishing, fitting), plus supervision time for technician coordination.
robot_success_rationale: Requires micron-level precision, shape judgment from impressions, hand-eye coordination for grinding/polishing, and aesthetic judgment. Humanoids lack the fine dexterity, surface texture perception, and domain expertise needed. Supervision requires social/linguistic skills and quality assessment.

task: Use complementary aids, such as infrared lamps, wet compresses, ice, and whirlpool baths to promote clients' recovery, relaxation, and well-being.
human_time_rationale: Applying infrared lamp, wet compress, ice pack, or running whirlpool bath for one client session, including setup and monitoring time.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine temperature sensing, safe handling of hot/cold therapies, positioning on client body, monitoring comfort and skin safety. Humanoids lack reliable thermal feedback and delicate tactile control for unstructured caregiving tasks.

task: Investigate crop, property, or habitat damage or destruction or instances of water pollution to determine causes and to advise property owners of preventive measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = investigating one damage/pollution incident on a property. Includes site visits, sample collection, analysis, and documentation. Typically 2–4 hours per case.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can traverse terrain, capture images, and collect samples in structured environments, but lack expertise in visual assessment, causal reasoning, environmental sampling protocol judgment, regulatory compliance, and communication with property owners. Limited outdoor navigation and fine dexterity for precise sample handling.

task: Perform tie spacing layout and measure, mark, drill or cut.
human_time_rationale: Laying out tie spacing, measuring, marking, drilling/cutting one tie set (~6–8 ties with spacing measurements and hole placement) takes ~15–25 min for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, spatial planning, and tool operation (drill/saw). Robot perception of spacing tolerances is limited; dexterity for steady tool use and marking is weak. High failure risk from misalignment or tool control errors.

task: Check for and remove moisture or contamination in damaged areas and keep areas dry until repairs are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting, drying, and monitoring a damaged area (e.g., water-damaged drywall, wet insulation, flooded basement section) until it reaches safe dryness.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can deploy dehumidifiers and air movers, but struggle with precise contamination detection (mold, salt deposits), moisture measurement assessment, navigating irregular damaged structures, and sustained monitoring over hours/days.

task: Cut and peel lead sheathing and insulation from defective or newly installed cables and conduits prior to splicing.
human_time_rationale: Cutting and peeling lead sheathing and insulation from a cable section; includes careful handling to avoid damage to inner conductors and safe removal of material. One instance = one cable/conduit segment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous control to cut sheathing without damaging inner conductors, precise peeling of brittle lead and insulation materials, and judging thickness/depth. Current robots lack fine manipulation precision and safety awareness for hazardous lead handling.

task: Clean and polish furniture and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: Time to clean and polish one piece of furniture or fixture (e.g., one table, one shelf) with cloth and polish, including drying.
robot_success_rationale: Robots handle wiping motions and basic dexterity moderately well, but polishing requires fine pressure control, material adaptation (cloth type, polish amount), and detecting uneven surfaces. Furniture shapes vary widely, complicating generalization.

task: Replenish supplies, such as drinking glasses, linens, writing supplies, and bathroom items.
human_time_rationale: Replenishing supplies in a typical space (hotel room, office, bathroom): gathering items from storage, transporting, and placing in designated locations.
robot_success_rationale: Robot can navigate and place objects on shelves/surfaces, but struggles with fragile items (glasses), folding/organizing linens, precise placement in confined bathroom spaces, and adapting to varied storage layouts. Perception and dexterity gaps limit reliable execution.

task: Prune or trim trees, shrubs, or hedges, using shears, pruners, or chain saws.
human_time_rationale: Pruning a typical shrub or small tree section; includes assessing branches, making cuts, clearing debris. One instance = one distinct plant or hedge section.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoids lack safe chain-saw operation, stable climbing/reach for tall trees, and refined pruning judgment (when/where to cut). Unstructured outdoor terrain and foliage occlusion further limit autonomy.

task: Assemble prototypes or models of circuits, instruments, and systems for use in testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one prototype/model circuit or instrument system. Includes soldering, wiring, component placement, and basic testing. Typical time for a skilled technician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (soldering, component placement), perception of small parts, and sequencing. Humanoids lack fine motor control for reliable soldering. Assembly of standardized components on jigs: ~40–80%; but circuit assembly demands precision below current capabilities.

task: Repair, fabricate, and install steel or wood fittings, using blueprints, shop sketches, and instruction manuals.
human_time_rationale: Skilled tradesperson: 2–3 hours per fitting, including blueprint review, layout, fabrication (cutting, drilling, welding/joinery), and installation with adjustments.
robot_success_rationale: Requires: interpreting 2D blueprints → 3D action, precision metalworking/woodworking (welding, saw cuts, drilling), spatial reasoning under constraints, tool changeover, and final fit-up. Humanoids lack fine motor control, tool competence, and reliable fabrication consistency.
